Q1: I kept things pretty simple with my study routine. I'd set aside blocks of time, usually around 25 hours a week, and I'd stick to that schedule regardless of what was happening in the course. I'm a visual learner, so I'd often print out diagrams or create my own charts to study from. The routine wasn't fancy, but it worked for me—I knew when I was going to study and I just did it without overthinking.
Q2: My pattern was pretty regular, actually. I'd study at the same times most weeks because that's just how I function best. Deadlines didn't really make me panic or change things up much because I trusted my routine to get me through. I might add an extra hour here or there before an exam, but nothing dramatic.
Q3: Honestly, I logged into the LMS mainly when I needed to check if there were new assignments posted or to download materials. I wasn't someone who browsed around or checked it constantly. Once I got what I needed, I'd log off and work with the materials on my own. It was more of a practical tool than something I spent a lot of time on.
Q4: I wouldn't say the LMS ever became a big part of my routine. I'd check it maybe a few times a week to make sure I wasn't missing anything important, but that was about it. My real routine happened offline with my printed notes and visual aids. The LMS was just the gateway to get the content I needed.
Q5: The video content was genuinely useful to me because I learn best by seeing things explained visually. I watched about 85 minutes worth, which helped me understand complex concepts. I also appreciated when lecture slides had good diagrams or flowcharts. Those visual resources stuck with me much better than walls of text.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums—I think I only posted once the whole course. It's not that I thought they were useless, but with my limited resources and time, I had to prioritize what directly helped me learn. Discussions felt like extra work that didn't fit my study style, so I focused on what I knew worked for me.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I'd be tested on or if it helped me understand a concept visually. I'm practical about these things—if something was going to help my exam performance, I'd do it. If it felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grades, I'd skip it without guilt.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different. I'd look at them and think, 'Is this going to help me pass?' If the answer wasn't a clear yes, I'd move on. Required activities got done because they had to be done, but optional ones had to prove their value to me first. With limited resources and a lot going on, I couldn't afford to waste time on extras.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. Most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download the PDFs and videos, then work with them on my own without being connected to the LMS. I'd create visual summaries, draw diagrams by hand, and review printed materials. The LMS would never capture how many hours I spent with a highlighter and my notebooks.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with course material but completely invisible to the system. I'd spend hours redrawing diagrams to memorize processes, or I'd review my handwritten notes repeatedly before exams. None of that showed up anywhere. My exam score of 97.52 came from that invisible work, not from clicking around the LMS.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video or document once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never touch the LMS version again. I preferred having materials saved locally so I could study without needing internet access. My one-time downloads turned into hours of offline study.
Q12: There were moments when life got in the way—having low resources means sometimes you're dealing with things other students don't worry about. But honestly, my stress stayed pretty low throughout. The CBT sessions I attended helped me keep perspective and not let challenges derail me. I'd just adjust and keep going with my routine.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected when I could access the LMS, which is partly why my attendance wasn't perfect. But I'd compensate by being efficient when I did engage. Fatigue sometimes hit, but my routine helped me push through. I learned to work smarter rather than harder because I didn't have the luxury of endless time or resources.
Q14: Sometimes I'd log in just to check something quickly and end up scrolling without really absorbing anything. Those moments happened when I was tired or distracted. But I recognized when that was happening and would log off rather than waste time. Real engagement for me happened offline with my visual materials.
Q15: I'll admit I postponed some assignments—my completion rate wasn't great at around 48%. Sometimes I'd prioritize exam preparation over smaller assignments because I knew where my strengths were. I'd think, 'I can ace the exam, so this assignment matters less.' It was a calculated decision, not laziness.
Q16: Deadlines would prompt me to check the LMS to make sure I knew what was due, but they didn't cause me stress. I'd note the deadline, factor it into my routine, and handle it. My response time averaged around 21 hours, which shows I wasn't last-minute but also wasn't rushing to submit immediately.
Q17: Feedback was helpful when I received it, but it didn't dramatically change my approach. If I got good feedback, I felt validated in my methods. If something needed improvement, I'd adjust my visual study materials accordingly. But I was already pretty confident in how I learned best.
Q18: The course structure gave me enough flexibility to work in my own way, which I appreciated. Having materials available to download meant I could fit studying around my other responsibilities. The structure didn't force me to be online constantly, which suited my offline study preferences perfectly.
Q19: Having visual content like videos and diagrams made engagement easier for me. What made it harder was when things required constant online presence or lots of discussion participation—that just wasn't my style. The course was designed well enough that I could succeed through focused, independent study.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for answers or trying to understand requirements without actually doing the work. Or they could be like me in reverse—very active online but struggling to translate that into completed assignments. Everyone has different strengths; mine was definitely exam performance over assignment completion.
Q21: That completely reflects my experience. My LMS activity was pretty low—minimal logins, barely any discussion posts—but I earned a Distinction. The system couldn't see my hours of offline visual study, my handwritten notes, or my diagram reviews. Low online activity doesn't mean low engagement with learning.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and what they clicked on. But they completely miss the depth of engagement. They can't measure understanding, retention, or the quality of offline study. My logs would show low activity, but my exam score tells a completely different story about my actual learning.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely understanding and connecting with the material, not just logging hours online. For me, engagement looks like creating visual summaries, really grasping concepts, and being able to apply knowledge on exams. It's about quality of learning, not quantity of clicks. I was deeply engaged even if the LMS couldn't see it.
Q24: The LMS would completely miss my visual learning process—all the diagrams I drew, the charts I created, the hours spent with printed materials. It wouldn't show my high resilience or the positive mindset I maintained throughout. It also couldn't capture how the CBT sessions helped me stay balanced. My real engagement story happened mostly offline.
